coredata.dc:title: Phenomenal improvement of external quantum efficiency, detectivity and responsivity of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot decorated zinc oxide nanorod/polymer schottky junction UV detector
coredata.dc:creator: Dhar, Saurab
Majumder, Tanmoy
Mondal, Suvra Prakash
coredata.dc:description: Photodetector properties of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot (NGQD) sensitized ZnO nanorods/poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS) Schottky junction has been investigated. The photodetector demonstrated superior external quantum efficiency (EQE) (∼57681% at −1V bias) at wavelength ∼340nm, which is 4 times higher than graphene quantum dot (GQD) modified ZnO NRs based detector and 35 times higher than pristine ZnO NRs detector. NGQD modified detectors also showed higher responsivity (∼158A/W), detectivity (8.7×1012 Hz1/2/W) at 340nm wavelength. The response and recovery times of the detector is very fast compared to GQD modified and pristine ZnO nanorod based detectors. Our UV detector also demonstrated higher photocurrent even at lower intensity range of incident light.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO nanorods
Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
PEDOT:PSS
Schottky diode
UV detector
External quantum efficiency (EQE)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Top view SEM micrograph of ZnO nanorods (b) The plane view transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of NGQDs before attachment. The high resolution TEM image is shown at the inset (c) Typical current density vs. bias voltage (J-V plot) of Sample S3 under dark condition. Schematic representation of sample S3 (inset). (d) J-V plot of sample S3 in semilogarithmic scale under dark and illumination of broadband light of intensity ∼80mW/cm2.
External quantum efficiency (EQE) % vs. wavelength plots of sample S1, S2 and S3. The carrier transport mechanism is shown at the inset.

(a) Transient photocurrent (J-t plot) of devices S1, S2, and S3 at bias voltage −1V under UV illumination of wavelength 340nm (b) Time response of photocurrent growth and decay curve (at insets) of sample S3.
Mott-Schottky plots of S1 and S3 samples under dark condition.
(a)The responsivity and (b) the detectivity for S1, S2 and S3 samples under bias voltage −1V.

the variation of photocurrent density (Jlight
− Jdark ) with UV light intensity (λ∼340nm) for S1, S2 and S3 samples.
Photodetector performances of ZnO based nanostructures and nanorods.

head.ce:title: Phenomenal improvement of external quantum efficiency, detectivity and responsivity of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot decorated zinc oxide nanorod/polymer schottky junction UV detector

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology, Agartala, 799046, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photodetector properties of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot (NGQD) sensitized ZnO nanorods/poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS) Schottky junction has been investigated. The photodetector demonstrated superior external quantum efficiency (EQE) (∼57681% at −1V bias) at wavelength ∼340nm, which is 4 times higher than graphene quantum dot (GQD) modified ZnO NRs based detector and 35 times higher than pristine ZnO NRs detector. NGQD modified detectors also showed higher responsivity (∼158A/W), detectivity (8.7×1012 Hz1/2/W) at 340nm wavelength. The response and recovery times of the detector is very fast compared to GQD modified and pristine ZnO nanorod based detectors. Our UV detector also demonstrated higher photocurrent even at lower intensity range of incident light.

ce:section.ce:para: ZnO nanorods (ZnO NRs) and NGQDs were synthesized using hydrothermal process [5,31]. For the growth of ZnO NRs, at first, chemically patterned fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) coated substrates (procured from Sigma Aldrich, surface resistivity∼ 10Ω/square) were thoroughly cleaned in DI water, acetone and isopropyl alcohol by ultrasonication. ZnO nanoparticle seed layer solution was prepared by adding 0.01M of zinc acetate dehydrate (Merck 99%) in 50mL isopropyl alcohol (Merck 99%) followed by heating and stirring at 65°C for 1h. During the heating and stirring process, 0.02M of diethanolamine (Merck 99.5%) was added to the previous solution. The solution was cooled down at room temperature and spin coated on cleaned FTO coated glass substrate 4–5 times followed by annealing at 200°C for 1hr and ZnO nanoparticle seed layers were formed. Afterwards, the seeded substrates were dipped in a hydrothermal reactor containing an equimolar solution of zinc nitrate (0.05M) (Sigma Aldrich 98%) and HMTA (0.05M) (Sigma Aldrich 99%). The reactor temperature was maintained at 90°C for 4h duration. The grown nanorods were cleaned with DI water and annealed at 400°C for 2h in air atmosphere.

For the synthesis of NGQDs, at first citric acid (1.26gm) and urea (1.08gm) were dissolve in 30mL DI water. The solution was poured in a Teflon lined Stainless Steel autoclave and heated up to 180°C for 8h. The as synthesized NGQDs were collected by adding ethanol and centrifuged at 9000rpm for 30min. The attachment was carried out by dipping the nanorods in aqueous solution of NGQDs for 2h followed by heating at 100°C. The photodetector device was fabricated by spin coating the PEDOT:PSS solution on NGQD sensitized ZnO nanorods at 1500rpm for 30s. Afterward, the samples were baked at 120°C for 10min. The active device area was 0.12cm2. Electrical contacts were made from bottom FTO and top PEDOT:PSS using a tiny amount of conducting silver paste.

Scanning electron micrographs (SEM) were recorded using a Nova Nano (FEI, USA) system. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out using a JEOL JEM-2100F microscope. Mott-Schottky (M-S) measurements were carried out using an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660D, USA). In electrochemical measurements, ZnO NRs were used as the working electrodes, a Pt wire as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as reference electrode. During the M-S measurements, 0.1M aqueous solution of Na2SO4 was used as the electrolyte. The current-voltage characteristics of the photodetctors were recorded out using a dual channel source meter (B2912A, Agilent, USA). The photoresponse measurements were carried out under the illumination of monochromatic light from a monochormator (Sciencetech, Canada) attached with a 270W Xe lamp as a broadband light source (Sciencetech, Canada).

Fig. 1 (a) shows the top view SEM image of ZnO nanorods grown vertically on FTO substrates. The average diameter and length of the nanorods have been found to be 100nm and 1μm, respectively. ZnO nanorods are single crystalline hexagonal wurtzite structure and oriented along (002) crystallographic direction. The detail growth and characteristics of ZnO nanorods has been reported elsewhere [5]. Fig. 1(b) represents the plane view transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of NGQDs before attachment. The as grown NQGDs are highly crystalline of average diameter 3–7nm. The high resolution TEM image (inset of Fig. 1b) shows the lattice spacing of 0.34nm, which is similar to (002) face of graphitic carbon [32]. To study the roles of NGQDs, we have characterized the following three samples. The samples are (i) FTO/ZnO NRs/PEDOT:PSS/Ag (sample S1) (ii) FTO/GQD sensitized ZnO NRs/PEDOT:PSS/Ag (sample S2) and (iii) FTO/N doped GQD sensitized ZnO NRs/PEDOT:PSS/Ag (sample S3). Fig. 1(c) represents the typical current density vs. bias voltage (J-V) characteristics of NGQD decorated ZnO nanorods/PEDOT:PSS Schottky junction under dark condition. Inset of Fig. 1(c) shows the typical device structure of NGQD decorated ZnO NRs/PEDOT:PSS Schottky junction UV detector. The J-V characteristics demonstrated excellent rectifying behaviour with rectification ratio ∼100 at ±1V. Fig. 1(d) represents the J-V characteristics of sample S3 under dark and illumination of white light of intensity 80mW/cm2. The reverse saturation current (Jdark ) enhances rapidly under illumination of light. As for example at −1V bias, the reverse current change (Jdark −Jlight ) is ∼79.1mA/cm2, which is ∼73 times higher than sample S1 and ∼6 times higher than sample S2. To determine the wavelength sensitive gain of the photodetectors, EQE measurements have been carried out at bias voltage −1V. The EQE can be expressed using the following equation [5,33]
(1)
E Q E
( % )
=
1240
J λ
λ
P
λ
× 100 %
Where, λ is monochromatic wavelength of light (nm), J λ is photocurrent density (mA/cm2) and Pλ is the intensity of light (mW/cm2) at λ. Fig. 2 shows the EQE vs. λ plots for samples S1, S2 and S3. The maximum EQE values for S1, S2 and S3 samples are obtained at 340nm and found to be 1617%, 13,167% and 57,681%, respectively. Interestingly, in all samples, the maximum EQE value enhances beyond 100%. The increase of EQE more than 100% of ZnO NRs/polymer Schottky junction can be explained using surface trap related carrier injection in nanorods [6,34]. Under reverse biased condition, the depletion width of ZnO NRs/polymer Schottky junction reduces due to hole-trapping at the interfaces. After a certain bias, such depletion layer thinning dominates electron tunneling at the junction. This phenomenon may cause EQE greater than 100%. More importantly, the significant enhancement of EQE for S3 compared to S2 sample is due to strong UV absorption, fast carrier transport and favourable band alignment at NGQD/ZnO nanorod interfaces [35]. Inset of Fig. 2 shows the schematic representation of carrier transport under reverse bias. The corresponding energy levels of ZnO NRs, NGQDs, FTO and PEDOT:PSS polymer have been utilized from the reported results [12,35]. Fig. 3 (a) shows the transient photoresponse behaviours of samples S1, S2 and S3. All measurements have been carried out at −1V bias under illumination of light of λ∼340nm with incident power 0.38mW/cm2. The on/off ratios of the samples S1, S2 and S3 are 3, 73 and 235, respectively. Interestingly, the on/off ratio for S3 detector is 3 times higher than S2 and 78 times higher than S1. Response and recovery times are important parameters for photodetectors. The response and recovery time have been estimated under turn-on and turn-off of UV light (λ =340nm) of intensity∼0.38mW/cm2. The rise and decay curves have been fitted by the following equations [36]
(2)
J R
= A
(
1 −
e
−
t
τ
1
)
+ B
(
1 −
e
−
t
τ
2
)
(3)
J D
= A
e
−
t
τ 1
+ B
e
−
t
τ 2
where JR and JD are the rise and decay photocurrent density, A and B are scaling constant, t is time when UV light was turned on and off, τ 1 and τ 2 are time constants. Fig. 3(b) shows the typical time response of photocurrent growth and decay curves of NGQD sensitized photodetector (S3). The fitted time constants during photocurrent growth are τ 1 ∼1.9s and τ 2 ∼5.4s. The same time constants for S1 and S2 samples are τ 1 ∼3.2s, τ 2∼ 11.4s and τ 1 ∼2.9s, τ 2 ∼5.9s, respectively. The fast and slow rise of photocurrent usually observed in ZnO nanorod based photodetectors [36–38].

The results showed that under illumination, there is a rapid growth of photocurrent followed by a slower component before saturation. In ZnO nanorods, it has been observed that, ambient oxygen molecules are adsorbed by capturing free electrons from the n-type surface
[
O
2
( g )
+
e −
→
O
2
−
(
a d
)
]
and a low conductive depletion layer is formed near the surface. When the photon of incident radiation is greater than the band gap energy of ZnO (which is about 3.37eV) electron-hole pairs are generated. Such band to band transition process is very fast and responsible for quick rise of photocurrent in the first few seconds. On the other hand, the photogenerated holes migrate to the surface along the band-bending, and release the physically adsorbed oxygen ions
[
h +
+
O 2 −
(
a d
)
→
O 2
( g )
]
, which results the decrease of depletion layer. This process also increases the current flow through the nanorod [5,34]. However, such photocurrent rise (τ 2) is comparatively slow compared to first part (τ 1). When the UV light is turned off, the photocurrent decays with faster and slower components. The decay processes was excellently fitted with a biexponential relaxation equation (Eq. (3)), with two relaxation time constants τ 1 ∼1.4s and τ 2 ∼2.8s (Inset of Fig. 3b). The faster one (τ 1) is attributed to the band-to-band recombination of photogenerated electrons with the holes in the bulk of the ZnO nanorods. However, there are plenty of electrons still remains on the surface of nanorods and these electrons are captured by the oxygen molecules by reabsorbing process and increases depletion layer, which results in a slow current decay (τ 2). The fast and slow decay time constants for S1 and S2 samples are τ 1 ∼8.5s, τ 2 ∼52.7s and τ 1 ∼7.1s, τ 2 ∼19.9s, respectively. Our results indicates that the time constants during rise and decay of photocurrent of ZnO nanorod/PEDOT:PSS schottky junction UV detectors can be improved by sensitizing the nanorods with GQDs and NGQDs, which is due to the reduce of surface traps as well as strong UV absorption. More importantly, we can achieve faster rise and fall time constants by decorating nanorods with NGQDs. Due to the favourable band alignment, NGQDs acts like an electron donor and enhances the effective carrier concentration of nanorods. The effective carrier concentration of NGQD decorated ZnO nanorods has been calculated using Mott-Schottky (MS) plots using a three electrode based electrochemical measurements. The M-S plots (
1
C
2
vs V
) for pristine ZnO and NGQD coated ZnO NRs are presented in Fig. 4 . The positive slope in linear region of MS plots for both samples, indicates the presence of donor type impurities. The donor concentration (Nd) was calculated from the MS plot using the following equation [31].
(4)
N d
= −
(
2
q ε
ε 0
)
(
d
(
1
C 2
)
d V
)
− 1
Where q, ε0 , є, V, C are electric charge, permittivity of free space, dielectric constant of ZnO, applied bias and junction capacitance, respectively. The donor concentration of ZnO NRs is found to be∼1.5×1019 cm−3, which is close to the reported results [31]. However, the Nd value for NGQD/ZnO NRs is 1.7×1020 cm−3. The improvement of carrier density occurs due to the attachment of NGQDs on nanorod surfaces. The fast rise and fall time of S3 sample is attributed to the enhancement of effective carrier concentration and reduction of surface trap states in ZnO nanorods. To study the performance of the photodetector, we have also studied the responsivity and the detectivity of the samples at −1V bias voltage. The responsivity (Rλ) and detectivity (Dλ ) of the samples has been determined using the following relation [5]
(5)
R λ
=
J
l i g h t
−
J
d a r k
P
o p t
(6)
D λ
=
R λ
2 q
J
d a r k
Where Jlight and Jdark denote the current density at light and dark. Popt is the intensity of incident light at a particular wavelength λ and q is the charge of electron. Fig. 5 shows the responsivity and detectivity of S1, S2 and S3 samples. Similar to EQE, the responsivity and detectivity for all samples have been found to be maximum at wavelength 340nm. The maximum responsitivity for S1, S2 and S3 samples are 3, 36 and 158A/W, respectively. On the other hand, the maximum detectivity for S1, S2 and S3 samples are 3.81×1011, 1.72×1012 and 8.75×1012 Hz1/2/W, respectively. The variation of photocurrent density (Jlight −Jdark ) with incident UV light intensity (λ=340nm) are shown in Fig. 6 . Interestingly, the photocurrent values in S3 sample is several times higher than S1 and S2 samples in all measured intensity range (0.07–0.31mW/cm2). This study also indicates the major role of NGQDs for strong UV absorption. Here, we have listed the photosensing performances of various ZnO nanostructures/PEDOT:PSS based junctions along with our experimental results in Table 1 . In comparison with others reported results, NGQD sensitized ZnO NR based detectors demonstrated superior UV detector performances in terms of photocurrent and responsivity.

In summary, we have reported NGQDs decorated ZnO nanorods/polymer Schottky junction UV detectors with high EQE (∼57681%), responsitivity (∼158A/W) and detectivity (∼8.75×1012 Hz1/2/W). The detector showed superior photocurrent with fast rise and fall time under on/off of UV light (λ∼340nm). Even very low intensity range of UV light (0.07–0.31mW/cm2), NGQD modified detector exhibited very high photocurrent compared to GQD modified and pristine ZnO NR based detectors. Our present study describes the advantages of N doped GQD as photosensitizer with ZnO nanorods for inorganic/organic Schottky junction UV detector applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This present research work was partially funded by CSIR Extramural Research Scheme, Sanction No. 03(1316)/14/EMR-II dated 16/04/2014, Government of India. We acknowledge the central research facility (CRF) of NIT Agartala for SEM, UV-VIS-NIR spectroscopy and XRD characterizations. We acknowledge Mr. K Debnath and MSDC laboratory, Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering, NIT, Agartala for electrochemical measurements.
